title: A Guide to the K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa Process

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Build a bulletproof I-129F petition by creating a master 'Relationship Timeline' that methodically organizes and references all evidence proving your relationship is genuine.
Proactively prepare your fiancé(e) for the consular interview by creating a concise 'Interview Briefing Book' that organizes your shared story and clarifies potential 'red flags'.
Upon your fiancé(e)'s U.S. arrival, immediately execute wedding logistics while simultaneously preparing the Adjustment of Status (green card) application to meet the strict 90-day marriage deadline.
Mitigate post-arrival financial stress by budgeting for a 3-6 month living expense 'runway' to cover the mandatory waiting period before your spouse can legally work.
summary: The K-1 visa process creates significant anxiety for couples due to its bureaucratic complexity and the high stakes of potential denial. This guide advises reframing the journey as a strategic project, empowering you to act as a project manager who meticulously documents your relationship, anticipates dependencies, and proactively mitigates risks. By adopting this structured, professional mindset, you can replace fear with confident control, ensuring you navigate the system with precision to successfully unite with your partner.
ai_overview_answer:
Successfully navigating the K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa process requires treating it as a multi-phase project focused on meticulous documentation and proactive planning. The process begins with filing Form I-129F, which must be supported by overwhelming evidence of a "bona fide relationship," such as a detailed relationship timeline and supporting exhibits. After USCIS approval, the case moves to a U.S. consulate, where the foreign fiancé(e) must complete Form DS-160 and pass a critical visa interview. Upon arrival in the U.S., the couple has a strict, non-extendable 90-day deadline to marry.

Immediately following the wedding, the next crucial step is to file Form I-485 to adjust status and apply for a green card. The U.S. sponsor must meet financial requirements, which increase to 125% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines during this final stage. It is essential to understand that the K-1 visa does not grant work authorization; this must be applied for separately with Form I-765 after marriage. Proactively managing these deadlines and documentation requirements is the key to a successful outcome.

@graph.description: Stop feeling anxious about the k-1 fiance visa process. This guide reframes the journey as a 5-phase project plan to help you build a bulletproof case.

step.name: Phase 1: The Project Kickoff — Building a Bulletproof I-129F Petition
step.text: Precision execution begins here, in the most critical phase of the entire process. The foundation for your success is Form I-129F, Petition for Alien Fiancé(e). Your objective is not merely to complete a document, but to submit an overwhelmingly convincing case file that eliminates ambiguity and minimizes the risk of a time-consuming Request for Evidence (RFE). Master the Critical Path by curating comprehensive evidence that proves you have a 'bona fide relationship.' Assemble a Compelling Case File by creating a master 'Relationship Timeline' document. Conduct Pre-Submission Due Diligence with a non-negotiable checklist to verify every core requirement.

step.name: Phase 2: Navigating the Mid-Project Gates — From NVC to Consular Interview
step.text: Your project now transitions from a domestic filing exercise into an international diplomatic process. Your objective is to flawlessly manage these administrative handoffs and prepare the beneficiary for the consular interview. Proactively Mitigate Interview Risk by creating a concise 'Interview Briefing Book.' Manage Project Dependencies by mapping critical dates on a calendar. Execute Flawless Documentation, especially the DS-160, with a zero-tolerance policy for errors.

step.name: Phase 3: The 90-Day Sprint — Managing the Critical Post-Arrival Deadline
step.text: Upon your fiancé(e)'s arrival, a 90-day sprint begins. Your objective is to execute a flawless wedding within the strict 90-day window and concurrently prepare the package for the Adjustment of Status. Treat the 90-Day Rule as a Hard Deadline by immediately securing a marriage license and booking a ceremony. Prepare the Adjustment of Status Packet in Parallel by assembling forms I-485, I-765, and I-131. Document Everything Meticulously, especially the I-94 Arrival/Departure Record.

step.name: Phase 4: The Final Deliverable — Securing Adjustment of Status & Beyond
step.text: With your AOS package submitted, the project enters its final phase. Your role evolves to include financial foresight and proactive career planning. Bridge the 'EAD/AP Gap' with proactive financial planning to cover living expenses during the waiting period for the work permit. Develop a Professional Integration Plan by overhauling resumes and networking. Understand the Conditions on the Green Card and be prepared to file Form I-751 to remove conditions on residence.

step.name: Phase 5: Capitalizing the Project — A CEO's Budget
step.text: Successfully navigating the final stages demands financial rigor. Create a comprehensive budget that anticipates every expense. Account for Core Government Fees (I-129F, DS-160, I-485), Third-Party & Logistical Costs (medical exams, translations, travel), and Post-Arrival & Transition Capital (marriage license, 3-6 month financial buffer). Consider optional costs like an attorney review for quality assurance.

mainEntity.name: What are the current processing times for the K-1 visa?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Total processing time from initial filing to visa issuance can range from 8 to 12 months, but this is highly variable. The timeline breaks down into distinct phases:

*   **Form I-129F Petition Approval:** This is the longest phase, often taking 8 to 11 months at USCIS.
*   **National Visa Center (NVC) Processing:** This typically takes 4 to 6 weeks.
*   **Embassy Interview & Final Processing:** This final stage can take another 2 to 3 months.

Always check the official USCIS website for the most current processing time estimates, as they change frequently.

mainEntity.name: What is the income requirement for a K-1 visa sponsor?

acceptedAnswer.text: The U.S. citizen sponsor must demonstrate financial capacity. For the initial K-1 visa stage, your income must be at least 100% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines for your household size. However, when you file for Adjustment of Status after marriage, the requirement increases to **125%** of the guidelines. These figures are updated annually, so you must check the current requirements on Form I-864P on the USCIS website.

mainEntity.name: What kind of questions are asked in the K-1 visa interview?

acceptedAnswer.text:
The interview is a verification milestone to confirm the authenticity of your relationship. Questions cover:

*   **Personal Background:** Your fiancé(e)'s work, education, and family.
*   **About the U.S. Sponsor:** Details about your job, family, and daily routine.
*   **Relationship History:** How you met, details of your engagement, and how you communicate.
*   **Future Plans:** Specifics about your wedding and where you will live.

The goal is a conversation to verify a genuine relationship. Honesty and consistency are paramount.

mainEntity.name: Can my fiancé(e) work in the U.S. on a K-1 visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. The K-1 visa does not grant the right to work. Your fiancé(e) must wait until after you are married and have filed the Adjustment of Status packet, which includes an application for an Employment Authorization Document (EAD). Working without authorization is a serious violation of U.S. immigration law.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa and a CR-1 Spousal Visa?

acceptedAnswer.text:
This is a crucial strategic decision based on whether you want to marry inside or outside the U.S. and your priority for work authorization.

| Feature | **K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa** | **CR-1 Spousal Visa** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Who It's For** | Engaged couples who will marry in the U.S. | Couples who are already legally married. |
| **Initial Entry** | Fiancé(e) enters; you must marry within 90 days. | Spouse enters as a permanent resident. |
| **Work Permit** | Cannot work on arrival. Must apply after marriage. | Can work immediately upon arrival. |
| **Green Card** | Must apply for a green card after marriage in the U.S. | Arrives in the U.S. with a green card. |
| **Timeline**| Generally faster initial processing for U.S. entry. | Longer initial processing, but the process is complete upon entry. |

mainEntity.name: What happens if we don't get married within the 90-day period?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 90-day rule is a hard, non-negotiable deadline with no extensions. If you do not marry within this timeframe, the K-1 visa expires, and your fiancé(e) falls out of legal status and must depart the United States. Overstaying is a serious immigration violation.

mainEntity.name: Can my fiancé(e) bring their children to the U.S.?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Unmarried children under 21 can accompany your fiancé(e) on a derivative K-2 visa. The children must be listed on the original Form I-129F petition. Each child will need their own K-2 visa application and must also file a separate Adjustment of Status application after you are married.

body.content: From Compliance Anxiety to Confident Control

The K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa process can feel less like a joyful step toward union and more like a high-stakes ordeal defined by bureaucratic ambiguity, long silences, and the constant fear that a single misstep could jeopardize your future. Most guides offer a checklist of forms, leaving you to manage the chaos and bear the emotional weight alone. This is not another guide; this is a strategic project plan.

We will reframe the K-1 visa journey into a 5-phase project management framework designed for you—the global professional, the CEO of this critical life project. It’s about trading anxiety for authority. Instead of reacting to government requests, you will learn to anticipate dependencies, manage timelines, and build a bulletproof case file that leaves no room for doubt. The objective is to proactively mitigate risks, from proving your bona fide relationship to preparing for the critical consular interview.

This framework equips you with the mindset to navigate US immigration with the same clarity you apply to your professional life. You will move from passive applicant to empowered project manager. Your goal is non-negotiable: to build a life with the person you love. This is your playbook to execute that project with precision and control.
Phase 1: The Project Kickoff — Building a Bulletproof I-129F Petition

Precision execution begins here, in the most critical phase of the entire process. The foundation for your success is Form I-129F, Petition for Alien Fiancé(e). Your objective is not merely to complete a document, but to submit an overwhelmingly convincing case file that eliminates ambiguity and minimizes the risk of a time-consuming Request for Evidence (RFE).
Master the Critical Path

Think of Form I-129F as the container. The real project deliverable—the task with the longest lead time—is curating the comprehensive evidence that proves you have a **"bona fide relationship."** Waiting until you’ve downloaded the form to start gathering this proof is a strategic error. The critical path involves beginning this process immediately. USCIS needs to see a clear narrative of your relationship. Start by compiling every piece of evidence that tells your story: flight itineraries from visits, communication logs, photos together, and written declarations from both of you detailing your history and future plans.
Assemble a Compelling Case File, Not Just a Pile of Photos

Resist the urge to dump a folder of photos and screenshots. A project manager organizes information for impact. Create a master "Relationship Timeline" document that serves as a table of contents for your evidence. This document should narrate the key milestones of your relationship and reference specific, labeled exhibits.

rows: **Exhibit A:** Flight itineraries and hotel receipts.
**Exhibit B:** Dated photos with family members.
**Exhibit C:** Photos of the engagement; screenshots of social media announcements.
**Exhibit D:** Sample communication logs showing consistent contact.

body.content: This structured approach makes the adjudicator's job easier, demonstrating the seriousness and authenticity of your commitment. You are presenting an organized, verifiable case, not a scrapbook.
Conduct Pre-Submission Due Diligence

Before mailing your petition, conduct a final quality assurance check. Simple administrative errors are a leading cause of rejections. Create a non-negotiable checklist to verify every core requirement.

items: **U.S. Citizenship Verified:** Is a copy of the petitioner's U.S. passport, birth certificate, or naturalization certificate included?
**Freedom to Marry Confirmed:** Are final divorce decrees, annulments, or death certificates for any previous marriages included for *both* partners?

**In-Person Meeting Rule Satisfied:** Have you included definitive proof, like airline tickets or passport stamps, that you met in person within the two years prior to filing? This is a strict requirement.
**Intent to Marry Documented:** Have you both signed written statements declaring your intent to marry within 90 days of the beneficiary's arrival in the United States?

body.content: Completing this phase with meticulous detail builds the momentum needed to navigate the subsequent phases with confidence.
Phase 2: Navigating the Mid-Project Gates — From NVC to Consular Interview

Your project now transitions from a domestic filing exercise into an international diplomatic process. Once USCIS approves your I-129F petition, the case is transferred to the Department of State's National Visa Center (NVC) and then routed to the U.S. embassy or consulate in your fiancé(e)'s country. Your objective is to flawlessly manage these administrative handoffs and prepare the beneficiary for the single most important milestone: the consular interview.
Proactively Mitigate Interview Risk

Success in this phase is defined by preparation, not performance. The consular interview is the ultimate quality gate. Equip your fiancé(e) to navigate it with clarity and confidence by creating a concise "Interview Briefing Book." This is not for memorizing scripts—a critical error that officers spot instantly. It is a tool to organize the facts of your shared story, ensuring your partner can speak honestly and consistently. Structure it with these strategic categories:

items: **Our Relationship Story:** A timeline of key dates, how you met, details of your trips, the engagement story, and future wedding plans.
**About the U.S. Petitioner:** Key biographical data, job details, family information, hobbies, and interests.
**Our Future Plans:** Where you intend to live, post-wedding plans, and how you will navigate the initial adjustment period.

**"Red Flag" Clarifications:** Proactively prepare honest, direct explanations for any circumstances that might invite extra scrutiny, such as significant age or cultural differences, a language barrier, or a short courtship.

body.content: As former Consular Officer Mandy Feuerbacher, Esq., notes, "The worst interviews I have encountered were the ones where it felt like pulling teeth to get any information out of the beneficiary." Your goal is to empower your fiancé(e) to tell your story, not offer terse answers that can be misinterpreted as evasiveness.
Manage Project Dependencies

A project manager lives by their timeline. In this phase, the sequence is rigid. You cannot schedule the consular interview without a case number from the NVC. You cannot attend the interview without the sealed results of a medical exam from an embassy-approved physician. Map these critical dependencies on a calendar the moment your petition is approved. This foresight allows you to book the medical exam promptly and gather required civil documents without introducing last-minute schedule risks.
Execute Flawless Documentation

The key deliverable for the beneficiary in this phase is the DS-160, the Online Nonimmigrant Visa Application. Approach this document with a zero-tolerance policy for errors. A single inconsistency between the DS-160 and the original I-129F petition can create doubt and lead to delays. Before submitting, conduct a rigorous data integrity check, cross-referencing every name and date against official documents. Once submitted, print the confirmation page; it is required for the interview. This meticulous quality control demonstrates the seriousness of your application and respects the officer's time.
Phase 3: The 90-Day Sprint — Managing the Critical Post-Arrival Deadline

The visa is stamped, and your fiancé(e) has arrived. This moment calls for celebration, but your project manager mindset must recognize that the most critical deadline of the entire process has just been triggered. U.S. entry is not the finish line; it’s the starting pistol for a 90-day sprint with zero tolerance for delay. Your objective is clear: execute a flawless wedding within the strict 90-day window and concurrently prepare the package for the final project phase, the Adjustment of Status.
Treat the 90-Day Rule as a Hard Deadline

From the moment your fiancé(e) is admitted into the U.S., a 90-day countdown begins. This is a rigid, legally mandated timeframe that cannot be extended. Failure to marry the U.S. citizen petitioner within this window results in the expiration of the K-1 visa, rendering your fiancé(e) "out of status." To manage this risk, your project plan must have a clearly marked "Day 1"—the date of entry stamped in the passport.
Immediately shift your focus to the logistical dependencies of marriage:

items: **Secure the Marriage License:** Research the specific requirements for the county where you will marry. Understand waiting periods, required documentation, and office hours.

**Book the Ceremony:** Whether planning a simple civil ceremony or a larger celebration, secure the date and officiant immediately. Government offices can have backlogs; leaving this to the final weeks is an unnecessary risk.

body.content: Prepare the Adjustment of Status Packet in Parallel

A savvy project manager prepares for the next phase before the current one ends. During this 90-day period, concurrently assemble the deliverables for the next milestone: obtaining a **green card**. This process, called Adjustment of Status (AOS), requires a new, comprehensive application package. By preparing these documents while you plan the wedding, you can file the moment the marriage certificate is in hand.

rows: Application to Register Permanent Residence or Adjust Status
The primary application for the green card, changing status from K-1 nonimmigrant to Lawful Permanent Resident.
An optional but critical form to apply for a work permit, enabling legal employment while the I-485 is processing.
Also optional, this form is for "Advance Parole," granting permission to travel internationally without abandoning the green card application.

body.content: Gathering supporting evidence for these forms—such as birth certificates, financial documents for the I-864 Affidavit of Support, and the new marriage certificate—is your primary task during this 90-day sprint.
Document Everything Meticulously

Your focus on documentation cannot wane. The evidence trail you build now is crucial for the AOS phase. Preserve all entry-related documents, especially the **I-94 Arrival/Departure Record**. This is the official proof of legal entry, and a copy is required for the AOS package. Access and print the electronic I-94 from the Customs and Border Protection (CBP) website. Continue to collect evidence of your shared life: photos of your wedding, receipts from joint expenses, and affidavits from guests. This continuous, professional approach to documentation will form the bedrock of your final submission.
Phase 4: The Final Deliverable — Securing Adjustment of Status & Beyond

With your AOS package submitted, the project enters its final, and perhaps most psychologically demanding, phase. This is not a time for passive waiting, but for a deliberate strategy to manage the transition until the ultimate deliverable—the **green card**—is secured. Your role as project manager now evolves to include financial foresight and proactive career planning to navigate the interim period with confidence.
Bridge the "EAD/AP Gap"

Upon filing Form I-485, you also file for two critical interim benefits: Form I-765 for an **Employment Authorization Document (EAD)**, or work permit, and Form I-131 for **Advance Parole (AP)** for international travel. You must treat the processing time for these documents as a planned "waiting period."

This creates a high-anxiety gap where the newly arrived spouse cannot legally work or leave the country. A project manager mitigates this risk with proactive financial planning. Before your fiancé(e) arrives, you should have a dedicated financial runway to comfortably cover all living expenses for a minimum of 3-6 months. This buffer is a core project requirement that transforms uncertainty into a manageable, planned phase.
Develop a Professional Integration Plan
This waiting period is a strategic opportunity, not a forced pause. Use this downtime to launch a proactive career integration plan.

items: **Resume & LinkedIn Overhaul:** Professional norms and keyword optimization for U.S. job markets are often different. Dedicate time to re-crafting the resume and LinkedIn profile to align with American hiring standards.

**Targeted Networking:** Identify professionals in your spouse’s field for informational interviews. The goal is to build connections, understand the industry landscape, and practice professional communication.
**Strategic Upskilling:** Use platforms like Coursera or LinkedIn Learning for targeted upskilling. A U.S.-based certification can be a powerful signal to future employers.

body.content: Understand the Conditions on the Green Card

When the green card is approved, it’s a monumental milestone, but it may not be the final one. If your marriage was less than two years old on the day of approval, your spouse will be granted a **conditional green card (CR1)**, valid for only two years. This is a final quality-control gate.

As the project manager, you must diarize the final task: jointly filing **Form I-751, Petition to Remove Conditions on Residence**. The filing window is strict: you must file during the 90-day period immediately before the conditional card expires. Missing this deadline can result in the termination of permanent resident status. Successfully removing the conditions marks the true completion of your multi-year project.
Phase 5: Capitalizing the Project — A CEO's Budget

Successfully navigating the final stages demands the same financial rigor you’d apply to a high-stakes business venture. A smart project manager accounts for all costs, not just the obvious government filing fees. Thinking like a CEO means creating a comprehensive budget that anticipates every expense, transforming potential financial stressors into manageable, planned costs.
Core Government Fees
These are the non-negotiable line items in your project budget, paid directly to U.S. government agencies.
*Note: Fees are subject to change. Always verify current amounts on the official USCIS and Department of State websites.*
Third-Party & Logistical Costs
Your budget must account for a range of variable expenses required to complete the process.

items: **Medical Examination:** Mandatory before the embassy interview. Costs vary by country but typically range from **$100 to $500**.
**Vaccinations:** Additional costs may apply if certain vaccinations are required.
**Document & Translation Services:** Gathering official documents like birth certificates and police clearances. Certified translations for non-English documents can cost **$20-$40 per page**.
**Travel & Accommodation:** For the visa interview at the U.S. embassy or consulate.
**Courier Fees:** For sending and receiving official documents.

body.content: Post-Arrival & Transition Capital
This is the most frequently overlooked part of the budget, yet it's the most critical for mitigating post-arrival stress.

items: **Marriage & Licensing:** Factor in the cost of a marriage license and any ceremony expenses.

**Financial Runway:** As detailed in Phase 4, you must capitalize your project with a dedicated **3-6 month financial buffer** to comfortably cover all living expenses while awaiting the work permit. This is a core project requirement.

body.content: Optional but Recommended Costs
For the risk-averse professional, one final cost is a high-value investment.

items: **Attorney Review:** Consider budgeting for a one-time consultation with an experienced immigration attorney to review your completed I-129F petition before filing. This provides an invaluable layer of quality assurance and can save you from costly errors and delays.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Gaining clarity on common process questions is essential for maintaining control. Here are direct answers to the most pressing concerns.

items:
**What are the current processing times for the K-1 visa?**
    Total processing time from initial filing to visa issuance can range from 8 to 12 months, but this is highly variable. The timeline breaks down into distinct phases:
    *   **Form I-129F Petition Approval:** This is the longest phase, often taking 8 to 11 months at USCIS.
    *   **National Visa Center (NVC) Processing:** This typically takes 4 to 6 weeks.
    *   **Embassy Interview & Final Processing:** This final stage can take another 2 to 3 months.
    Always check the official USCIS website for the most current processing time estimates, as they change frequently.

**What is the income requirement for a K-1 visa sponsor?**
    The U.S. citizen sponsor must demonstrate financial capacity. For the initial K-1 visa stage, your income must be at least 100% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines for your household size. However, when you file for Adjustment of Status after marriage, the requirement increases to **125%** of the guidelines. These figures are updated annually, so you must check the current requirements on Form I-864P on the USCIS website.

**What kind of questions are asked in the K-1 visa interview?**
    The interview is a verification milestone to confirm the authenticity of your relationship. Questions cover:
    *   **Personal Background:** Your fiancé(e)'s work, education, and family.
    *   **About the U.S. Sponsor:** Details about your job, family, and daily routine.
    *   **Relationship History:** How you met, details of your engagement, and how you communicate.
    *   **Future Plans:** Specifics about your wedding and where you will live.
    The goal is a conversation to verify a genuine relationship. Honesty and consistency are paramount.

**Can my fiancé(e) work in the U.S. on a K-1 visa?**
    No. The K-1 visa does not grant the right to work. Your fiancé(e) must wait until after you are married and have filed the Adjustment of Status packet, which includes an application for an Employment Authorization Document (EAD). Working without authorization is a serious violation of U.S. immigration law.

**What is the difference between a K-1 Fiancé(e) Visa and a CR-1 Spousal Visa?**
    This is a crucial strategic decision based on whether you want to marry inside or outside the U.S. and your priority for work authorization.

**What happens if we don't get married within the 90-day period?**
    The 90-day rule is a hard, non-negotiable deadline with no extensions. If you do not marry within this timeframe, the K-1 visa expires, and your fiancé(e) falls out of legal status and must depart the United States. Overstaying is a serious immigration violation.

**Can my fiancé(e) bring their children to the U.S.?**
    Yes. Unmarried children under 21 can accompany your fiancé(e) on a derivative K-2 visa. The children must be listed on the original Form I-129F petition. Each child will need their own K-2 visa application and must also file a separate Adjustment of Status application after you are married.

rows: Engaged couples who will marry in the U.S.
Couples who are already legally married.
Fiancé(e) enters; you must marry within 90 days.
Spouse enters as a permanent resident.
Cannot work on arrival. Must apply after marriage.
Must apply for a green card after marriage in the U.S.
Arrives in the U.S. with a green card.
Generally faster initial processing for U.S. entry.
Longer initial processing, but the process is complete upon entry.

body.content: Conclusion: You Are in Control

The K-1 visa process is an intricate, high-stakes endeavor. But the core of this framework is a shift in perspective: you are not a passive applicant hoping for the best. You are the project manager, applying the same skills you use to drive successful outcomes in your professional life.

This is not about blind optimism; it is about leveraging a structured approach to impose order on a bureaucratic system. You know how to identify critical path items, manage stakeholders, and mitigate risk. You are simply applying a proven methodology to a new, and deeply personal, domain.

This mindset does more than organize paperwork. Its most profound benefit is emotional. By focusing on the elements you can control—the quality of your petition, the thoroughness of your documentation, the preparation for each milestone—you systematically reduce the power of the unknown. You replace fear with a clear action plan, protecting your peace of mind and your relationship from the immense stress this journey can impose.

The goal isn't just a visa; it is to begin your life together on a foundation of teamwork and shared accomplishment. You have the blueprint. You understand the five phases. The system is the environment, but you define the strategy. You are in control. Now, go execute your project.
